title: Why Is My Cat Licking Concrete or Walls? Causes and Veterinary Triage
meta_desc: Is your cat licking concrete? Learn about pica, anemia, and when this behavior requires a vet visit. Urgent care guide for cat owners.
content_html:
What Is This Behavior?

When a cat licks non-food items like concrete, bricks, or walls, it is generally referred to as a form of pica. This behavior involves an appetite for substances that are largely non-nutritive. In many cases, cats are drawn to the cool temperature, the unique texture, or the trace minerals found in these materials. However, it is often an outward sign of an internal physiological need or a behavioral response to stress.

Is It an Emergency?

The urgency level for a cat licking concrete or walls is Medium. While this behavior is rarely an immediate life-threatening emergency, it is often a clinical sign of underlying medical issues such as feline anemia, nutritional deficiencies, or chronic kidney disease. If you notice your cat has pale gums, extreme lethargy, or is refusing their regular food alongside this licking behavior, you should contact a veterinarian within 24 hours.

Why a Photo or Video Helps Triage

Providing a photo or video of your cat performing the behavior can be a vital tool for your veterinary team. A video allows the vet to see if the licking is compulsive or if it seems to be triggered by specific environmental factors. Additionally, a clear photo of your cat's mouth and gums can help the triage professional check for signs of anemia (pale or white gums), which is a common cause for this specific type of pica.

faqs.question: Can anemia cause a cat to lick walls?
faqs.answer: Yes, anemia is one of the most common medical reasons for this behavior. Cats with low red blood cell counts may instinctively seek out minerals like iron or calcium from concrete or dust.

faqs.question: Is licking concrete dangerous for my cat?
faqs.answer: While the concrete itself isn't typically toxic, licking it can lead to the ingestion of harmful bacteria, cleaning chemicals, or debris. It can also cause wear and tear on the tongue over time.

faqs.question: Could this be a sign of boredom?
faqs.answer: Yes, pica can sometimes be behavioral. If a cat is under-stimulated or anxious, they may develop repetitive licking habits as a way to self-soothe or pass the time.
